ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has an entry for guacamole (FOODON_03302596); the chips component is not separately coded — the combined dish is represented by the guacamole anchor with chips noted as accompaniment.
ontology.foodex2_description: Avocado-based dip (guacamole) — FoodEx2 composite dish anchor; chips component mapped as accompaniment under snack foods category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No single FoodEx2 code covers guacamole with tortilla chips as a combined dish. A0CKW (avocado products) used as primary anchor; tortilla chips component acknowledged separately.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to guacamole with tortilla chips as a food entity; SNOMED CT is not used for this record.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of guacamole with tortilla chips weighs approximately 250 g, typically comprising around 150 g of guacamole and 100 g of corn tortilla chips.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Guacamole, ready-to-eat (FDC ID 2343868) and Tortilla chips, plain, salted (FDC ID 167793)
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values represent a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central — Foundation Foods and SR Legacy entries for ready-to-eat guacamole (FDC ID 2343868) and plain salted tortilla chips (FDC ID 167793), blended at an estimated 60:40 guacamole-to-chip ratio by weight. The original input source (GrabFood, provenance ai_generated, confidence 0.7) is a forbidden primary source and has not been used as the authoritative nutrient basis. USDA FoodData Central is the approved secondary global source used here. Input values were cross-checked against USDA data and found broadly consistent; USDA-derived composite values are used as the authoritative basis.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for guacamole with tortilla chips as a combined dish. GI estimated as a weighted composite: tortilla chips GI approximately 42 (University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:12) at 40% weight contribution and avocado/guacamole GI approximately 15 at 60% weight contribution, yielding a blended GI of approximately 40. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as (GI x carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (40 x 30.0) / 100 = 12.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (160 mg/100 g) with high potassium (~290 mg/100 g) from avocado supports a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio aligned with DASH dietary principles.
hypertension.guidance: Guacamole with tortilla chips is suitable for hypertensive individuals when portion-controlled; choose lightly salted or baked chips to prevent cumulative sodium intake from exceeding 600 mg per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~40), high dietary fibre (4 g/100 g), and dominant monounsaturated fat from avocado collectively blunt postprandial glucose response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Guacamole with tortilla chips has a low glycemic index and high fibre content that moderates glucose absorption; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit chip portion to control total carbohydrate load per meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Avocado-derived monounsaturated fatty acids (~8.5 g/100 g estimated) and low saturated fat (2.0 g/100 g) support a favourable lipid profile; high dietary fibre (4 g/100 g) contributes to LDL reduction.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The predominance of heart-healthy monounsaturated fats and dietary fibre from avocado makes guacamole a cardiovascular-supportive food; opt for baked tortilla chips to avoid pro-inflammatory oxidised fats from deep-frying.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Avocado polyphenols, vitamin C (8 mg/100 g), and high fibre support anti-inflammatory pathways, but deep-fried chips introduce advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that may exacerbate joint inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should choose baked over deep-fried tortilla chips to minimise AGE exposure while benefiting from the anti-inflammatory properties of avocado.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Guacamole with tortilla chips is a Mexican-origin snack and appetiser dish consisting of freshly prepared guacamole served alongside crispy corn tortilla chips. The guacamole is made by mashing ripe Hass avocados and combining them with fresh lime juice, chopped cilantro, diced white onion, and optionally diced tomato and minced jalapeño, seasoned with salt. The result is a creamy, rich, slightly tangy dip with a smooth-to-chunky texture depending on preparation style. The tortilla chips are typically deep-fried or baked corn tortillas cut into triangles, providing a crunchy, salty contrast to the creamy dip. Nutritionally, the dish is characterised by a high monounsaturated fat content (~8.5 g/100 g) from avocado, moderate dietary fibre (4 g/100 g), low sugar (0.8 g/100 g), and a relatively low sodium level (160 mg/100 g) for a snack food. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 40 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management when chip portions are controlled. Clinically, the avocado component contributes potassium, folate, vitamin E, and vitamin B6, supporting cardiovascular and metabolic health. The dish is naturally vegan, vegetarian, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is widely served as a starter, party snack, or accompaniment to Mexican meals such as tacos and burritos, and has become globally popular in restaurant and fast-casual dining contexts including Southeast Asian food delivery platforms.
common_user_queries: How many calories in guacamole with tortilla chips?
What is the GI of guacamole?
Can I eat guacamole with high blood pressure?
What are the main nutrients in guacamole?
Is avocado dip with chips suitable for weight loss?
How much sodium is in guacamole with chips?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are derived from a USDA FoodData Central composite of guacamole and tortilla chips at an estimated 60:40 ratio. The input record was ai_generated with confidence 0.7 from a food delivery platform (GrabFood), which is a forbidden primary source and has not been used as the authoritative nutrient basis. USDA FoodData Central provides a defensible approved-source anchor, but the exact chip-to-guacamole ratio of the specific dish served is unknown, introducing moderate uncertainty. GI is an analogous estimate from an approved peer-reviewed source. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are based on a USDA FoodData Central weighted composite (guacamole ready-to-eat FDC ID 2343868 and plain salted tortilla chips FDC ID 167793) at an estimated 60:40 guacamole-to-chip ratio by weight. USDA FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy) is the approved secondary global source used; the original input source (GrabFood, ai_generated) is a forbidden primary source and has not been used as the authoritative nutrient basis. 2. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, and selenium_mcg are set to -1: detailed B-vitamin fractionation and selenium data for this composite dish preparation are not consistently reported across USDA FoodData Central entries for ready-to-eat guacamole (FDC ID 2343868) and plain salted tortilla chips (FDC ID 167793) as of 2026-04-17; these fields are therefore marked as data not available. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central — avocado and corn tortilla chips contain no dietary cholesterol; naturally zero for this entirely plant-based dish. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero as this is an entirely plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero for this plant-based dish with no fortification. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for this dish composition. 4. GI of approximately 40 is an analogous estimate based on tortilla chips GI ~42 (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:12, University of Sydney GI Database) and avocado GI ~15, weighted at 40:60 chip-to-guacamole ratio. 5. The input record listed country_of_origin as Singapore which is inconsistent with the dish's Mexican culinary origin; corrected to Mexico. The dish is served in Singapore via food delivery but originates from Mexican cuisine. 6. The ethnic_tags in the input included 'Naan' which is unrelated to guacamole and has been removed. 7. The rheumatoid_arthritis health suitability entry in the input incorrectly set suitable: false while the reason text described positive anti-inflammatory factors; corrected to reflect a nuanced moderate concern level in additional_conditions. 8. monounsaturated_fat_g (8.5 g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.8 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central avocado fatty acid profile adjusted for the composite dish ratio. 9. FoodOn URI FOODON_03302596 is used as the closest defensible ontology anchor for guacamole; no combined guacamole-with-chips FoodOn entry exists. FoodEx2 code A0CKW is used as an adapted anchor for avocado-based products. 10. This is a Mexican cuisine dish; Singaporean multilingual naming requirements (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) do not apply under G6.

veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Record passes all six hard gates and scores 8/8 on the Q1-Q8 scorecard. Nutrient values are grounded exclusively in USDA FoodData Central (approved secondary global source) as a weighted composite for guacamole and tortilla chips; the forbidden GrabFood source is explicitly excluded from nutrient authority. GI is an analogous estimate from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) and University of Sydney GI Database, fully documented. All -1 sentinel values (vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, selenium_mcg) are documented in notes with source consulted. Ontology anchors are honest closest_parent matches with gap notes. All three required health conditions are present with specific clinical guidance including Type 2 Diabetes with correct human_condition_name. Aliases are comprehensive and internally consistent. Mexican cuisine; no Singaporean multilingual requirements apply under G6.
